top_rant.text:
I just received this welder today and am posting my initial impression after putting it through its paces with test strips, not having used it on live cells yet:

The manual is printed in Chinese with no English translation, and I cannot find manuals for download on Sunkko's site, so this is disappointing. It was easy enough to discern how the various features work.

I tested welding to the test patch, with the included nickel-plated strips and with a coil of 0.2mm nickel and it did a perfectly good job; when I pulled the strip away the weld remained behind. I then used the welder to build up the positive lead with several layers of nickel on three flat-top cells in a flashlight where I had finicky connections (button-tops would have been more appropriate) and it did a perfectly good job.

---
I'm keeping this updated as I work on my welder. It has snowballed to the point where I am going to be rewinding the transformer, which is wound with a CCA bar -- or really CPA, since they appear to have used copper paint or gilding rather than plating(!!!)
---
For an easier fix, I looked closer at the front terminals; it's just copper strands shoved into the connector and an under-size screw which is apparently to clamp down on the cable but does not. Instead of removing those terminals, simply solder the wire into the terminal. I just received a 737G+ and found that the termination point of the cables is where these losses are incurred.

The finely-stranded OFC wire is not the problem nor is it crappy; fine-stranded OFC wire actually costs a premium over solid or coarse-stranded wire, so you do not need to replace the cable nor do you need to re-coil the transformer. That both the fixed head and pen tap off of the same coil, and are actually using the same cable and the fixed head works perfectly is evidence of that. The difference is the connections in the fixed head are actually crimped with the crimp being clamped upon to make the fixed head connection, while the pen electrodes just have the cable shoved into them and a simple screw plunges into the cable end but really does not improve the connection by a measurable amount.

To fix:

Remove the screws from the front terminators/jacks, push the jacks off the cables, and out through the front. Put the jacks in a vise, and tin the interior of the electrode. Then, unbolt the transormer to give yourself some slack, push the cables out through the front of the unit, twist the end tight to compact it, and coat the interior of the jack and the table itself with flux, generously, and use propane blowtorch to heat the terminator, and touch the solder (I'm using conventional 60/40 on mine for its melt and flow characteristics) to the cable and jack, and it will be drawn into the connector. Remove heat, let the jack cool, and once the solder has solidified (you'll see the color and lustre change in an instant when the solder freezes) you can then use a spanner/wrench as a heatsink or other means to bring the temp down quickly, and then you can shove the jack back into the unit, then do the same to the other electrode. Also note, there is an additional lead coming off of the welding pen jacks on the 737G+, so you will need to make sure you reconnect this; on mine I am going to simply insert it into the cable and use a soldering iron to solder the connection once it is completed. I'm 99.9995% confident this will solve the problem as I can measure the resistance change when I nudge these cables where they insert into the jacks.

----

Mod completed, and the behavior is better (when the pen is cool), and resistance from one terminal to the other is now under 1ohm. The resistance of my multimeter leads is 0.5ohm, so the resistance of the coil from terminal to terminal is 0.5ohm.

The way the coil is terminated into the jack terminals is the end of the coil are wrapped in a copper foil (yes, it's foil - I did not grab my calipers but I would be very surprised if the copper sheet is even 24ga) which is then shoved into the jack, and then a small screw is inserted through the terminal, to pierce the foil and realistically does very little to improve the connection. It's a very crappy implementation. I took the jack out, etched it with acid then tinned it, and then I soldered the cables into the terminals.

However, each lead from contact to electrode tip is around 1ohm apiece, resulting in 3 ohms total resistance in the circuit.

Lo and behold, I initially had several successful welds in a row with the pen with the welder set to 50%@2p. Once the pen heated up, I could no longer get successful welds of 0.2mm Nickel to nickel. Nickel to steel is still no problem. I now get more successful welds, before the welding pen heats up. Once the pen is warm, no more nickel-to-nickel welds until I let the pen cool. The pen needs to be redesigned.

Mod, pen rebuild:

I disassembled the pen, took out the electrodes and put them in a vise, heated them with a prpopane blowtorch, and then I fed the electrode+cable joint with as much solder as it would suck in, and then reassembled the pens, leaving the cooling vents uncovered but wrapping the rest with kapton tape to hold the pen together.. I can now weld at 12P@70% even .20mm nickel to .20mm nickel, but because I had overdone it a little with the solder (some sucked up into the cable so it inteferes with the spring) the retraction feature is lost. I already had a new pen on the way but I was so excited by the progress I made that I would follow through.

Now the entire loop electrode to electrode through the transformer is only about 1.5 ohm warm while previous to soldering the joints, each electrode was 1.5ohm apiece when cool, and it increased by about 5x when warm (not even hot). So, I basically reduced the cold resistance from 4.5 ohm to 1.5 ohm, with much of the resistance at the jack-to-cable connection, where the "O" plug is still not soldered (I wanted to try to save the boot intact) and the O plug is still used rather than hardwiring the pen.

The pen jacks and pen itself both need to be redesigned. Copper foil is NOT a proper crimp, and a simple metal screw is not a proper clamping method - and the screw appears (by eye and feel; I did not do a spark or corrosion test) to be iron or mild steel, not even stainless or nickel.

----
Followup a couple weeks later:

I've noted some improvement to the point where the handheld probes will blow holes in nickel on 2p@99%, but whenever the welder warms up (only a few spot welds) current drops by a couple hundred amps - this is after only a few welds of 0.2mm nickel. While splicing OFC welding cable to the leads that are crimped and soldered to the transformer taps, I noticed copper is so thin that it burns off and green coloring of the flame disappears (proving the copper clad is ultra-thin) the leads melt easily with a standard propane torch, and when I tried to twist them, it behaved like CCA rather than OFC. I crimped the end of the lead and put the crimp in a vise to file it to verify my suspicion, and sure enough - no sparks, plus filing down the cross-section reveals a silvery aluminium sheen, with no traces of copper (remember, I'd burned off the copper and the flame no longer had a green tint). The stock cabling in the welder is copper clad aluminium!

Looking into it further, I unwrapped the secondary coil, and while it does have 3.5 turns, it is a flat bar, and when I scratched it, what appears to be copper _PAINT_ chipped off, revealing aluminium underneath. Also the solder joint in the crimp holding the lead to the tap is globular, indicating a cold solder joint. Given that Aluminum starts out with 55% more resistance and the resistance vs. temperature curve is steeper than that of copper's, it's not difficult to see why these welders struggle.

So, let's list the problems, shall we?

* the jacks are poorly secured to the internal leads
* the secondary winding is aluminum
* the leads coming off the secondary taps are CCA - with an _extremely_ stingy copper cladding
* The hand probe leads are CCA
* The connections in the hand probe are just awful as the loose, high-resistance connections from the lead to the probe jacks
* The fixed welding head suffers from the same flaws, but due to the shorter length of aluminium to the connections and the smaller number of loose, high-resistance connections, it works fine until you've had a very long welding section

As such I'm going to be cutting out the secondary winding from the transformer, and will be replacing it with 4ga OFC cable. I am debating whether to grind/polish/gold plate the probe jacks, design and 3D print a new front panel and incorporate low-resistance MIG/TIG probe connections designed for high amperage use, or design a breakout box with MIG/TIG terminals. In any case I am poopcanning the aluminum cabling.

-----
Mod complete, welder is still anemic. Quite honestly, this does not live up to even warranty of merchantability because this welder is so flawed. I bought a BATTERY POWERED welder that outperforms this sunkko heap of junk.

I tried a Pedi-Paws when they first came out. I'm a Certified Dog Trainer, Vet Tech & a Dog Groomer. The concept of the Pedi-Paws is a great one & made perfect sense to me. Problem was, it is just not powerful enough, even for my small Jack Russell "Terrorists". I was really bummed out about it too.

Then one day my Dad came home from a local hardware/home improvement store with this product! BEST INVESTMENT IN THE WORLD for me!! It works WONDERFULLY on dogs nails!

It's powerful enough to do the job quickly without putting the dog thru the torture of holding them there while these inferior (my opinion, again) products that don't have strong enough motors to do the job quickly & well.

I have 2 Jack Russell "Terrorists", one HATES having her feet handled & the other, who's been very fearful of anything new since she was 12 weeks old. After introducing them to the Dremel I mentioned, my JRT who hates to have her feet handled, will now let me use the dremel I have to do her nails, (she still doesn't like it, but she now lays still & lets me get it done.) If I get the old fashioned nail clippers out, she runs for the hills. My other, fearful JRT loves it!
Once she got used to it, she now lays on her back & actually falls asleep while I'm grinding away at her nails!

The model I bought, came with a carry case too. I love it, because I can also keep combes, hemostats & lots of other "doggie" items in it.
It's become my "Doggie Doctor Bag"! I got my Dremel at a local hardware/home improvement store & it's much cheaper than the "Pet Grooming" Dremels I've seen here. Because it comes with lots of extras, especially the case, which is worth it's weight in gold!

I was looking for a replacement battery for my Dremel & saw all the other Doggie Nail Grooming products & just had to let all of you folks know that you can save money & get a BETTER model Dremel or about the same & sometimes, LESS money than the inferior ones that are just packaged differently to draw in pet owners! They get the job done better, much quicker with a faster RPM that won't bog down & most dogs will happily tolerate, because it takes less time!!

What I did to get my dogs used to these, was (BEING PATIENT WITHOUT GETTING ANGRY IS THE KEY HERE FOR SUCCESS FOLKS!

1. When I first brought it home, I attached the battery & set it on the floor, to let the dogs "approach & retreat" at their own pace. Dogs always use this method when they are unsure. They will approach something new & then retreat, checking to see if it's safe.
After the first couple times of approaching it & they actually started to sniff it, I used a clicker, but you can also say "Good Dog" AS THEY ARE SNIFFING IT! (Timing is SO IMPORTANT) then treat them. Do this as many times as you need to, until they get more comfortable & get to the point that no matter WHERE you set it on the floor (put it in a different room on the floor, so they will get used to it in different rooms. Dogs don't generalize well.) Do this until they get to where it doesn't seem to be such a "weird" thing to them when they see it. You'll know when, they will start to pay less & less attention to it. VERY IMPORTANT ~ DON'T turn it on yet!

2. When they are used to seeing it sitting on the floor around the house & seem to be OK with it. (You'll know), they'll pay less & less attention to it. It's become a part of the 'scenery'. Now pick it up (STILL DON'T TURN IT ON!!) and rub it on their bodies. Just for a second or so & if they are calm about it (they can look at & sniff it, which is GOOD), praise them I give them a treat. Then work on lengthening the time you rub it on their bodies (with the side of the Dremel) & treat each time they are calm about it. If at ANY TIME they seem stressed or try to run away, back off & go back to shorter contact times. Make sure you stay calm yourself. Use a 'sing-song' or happy voice when talking to them & DON'T FORGET REALLY YUMMY TREATS!!

Next turn it on in another room. Sit on the floor & hold it, LETTING THE DOG APPROACH YOU! NEVER go towards them with it, drag them to it, or hold it out towards them! When they start coming a little closer (remember, this is a new sound, so they will keep their distance, but if they don't run away, toss a nice yummy treat to them that they can get without coming closer the first time. WAIT & LET THEM APPROACH YOU! It's a good idea to have the best, smelliest treats that your dog loves with you (Make sure they know you have treats before you turn it on. Let them get a good whiff of the treat too. As you toss the treats to the dog, try to get the dog to take just one more step a little closer by tossing the treat so it lands a little closer to you each time.

This takes patience, but BELIEVE me folks, the benefits outweigh the time this takes initially. But in the long run, you'll be SO GLAD you did it!

When you can get the dog comfortable enough to come close, let the dog smell it. Maybe rub something nice & smelly on the outside casing, like a hot dog or some other food your dog LOVES!.(Keep the sanding band away from their body & don't let it get tangled in any long hair!

The goal here is to get the dog to come close to you WHILE the Dremel is running, on his own & eventually, rub the Dremel housing on the dogs body while it's running.

It's helpful to have someone to help you & feed GREAT treats while you are doing that. The dog will develop GREAT associations with the running Dremel, which is your goal! After a time & your dog will let you know when he/she is ready, to use it on toe nails. Quickly take just one "swipe" over the end of ONE NAIL & immediately let the foot go! Praise your dog like he/she just won you a million dollar lottery! Then feed some AWESOME treats & give MORE PRAISE!!

Many dogs are too intimidated still to lay down & let you handle their feet with the dremel running at first. That's OK. Pick up a paw & just barely do the end of one nail at first. Work your way up to 2 nails, eventually doing a whole paw at one time. Make sure to reward LAVISHLY! You know your dog, so if you think that he/she is ready to try to do more nails at one time, go for it. Just don't push for too much too fast! I promise you, the patience will pay off!

It WILL work if you invest the time to intitially introduce them correctly, WITH OUT FORCING THEM at any time. The years that will follow that you are able to get the nails filed down well in a very short amount of time, because you are not fighting with a dog pulling their feet away from you constantly, it's WELL WORTH HOWEVER LONG IT TAKES!!

Believe me, I KNOW it works! My JRT was a very fearful puppy when I got her at 12 weeks old. I've NEVER had a dog, especially that young, that was so afraid of EVERYTHING! She's going to be 3 the beginning of November & I've very patiently gotten her over 99% of her phobias. She was afraid of EVERYTHING! Sheesh!!)

If anyone needs any other advice or help, please feel free to leave a comment. I will be notified of any comments left on this post & I will respond. I'll GLADLY HELP IN ANY WAY I CAN!!

This is a super over-simplified version, but I think you get the idea.
Good Luck Everyone!!

Since Amazon is currently out of this particular Dremel (with the bag) here is a link to the same Dremel, but this one is more expensive, because it has an end or adapter that you would never need if you are only buying this for doing your dogs nails.

I had a hard time spending $600 for this but after all my research, I thought it was probably the best unit on the market for a home distiller. The construction quality is very good. Most all the parts are made of stainless steel. All the parts are serviceable / replaceable. I wish it made a full gallon of water. I have had it for several months now and would not want to do without it. I have four people in my household and we are making water 3-4 times a day. We find it difficult to keep up with our demand. However, as a result, everyone is drinking more water and we no longer buy bottled water. We have purchased several stainless steel water bottles to take water with us on the go.

We now find it hard to drink water at a restaurant somewhere because of the chlorine taste. Call me crazy, but I would swear that since drinking nothing but distilled water, my joint pain is pretty much gone. I wish we had two of these. My father liked ours so well that he purchased one for himself. The only complaint I have is that it makes about 3/4 gallon of water and I wish it made a full gallon or a little more. However, if the unit was any larger it probably would be too big to sit on our kitchen counter. I would definitely purchase this again and wish I had a second one to keep up with our household demand.

Also, here is something I would like to point out to everyone. I purchased extra charcoal filters with the unit. I noticed right away that the filter that came with the unit was stitched up using cotton thread around the outside edge. The extra filters I purchased were obviously made by another company because the outside edges had been glued. It concerns me that we go to all this trouble to purify the water and then the filters have been glued with who knows what kind of glue. They also did not contain as much charcoal as the original filter. Therefore, I will be only purchasing my replacement filters from the manufacturer since there is a definite quality difference between filters. The unit also puts out a lot of heat and makes it hot in our kitchen. This is a negative in the summer but should be a positive in the winter time. Our power bill has been unusually high but I can't say if it was caused by all the water we have been making or just because it has been so hot outside and our A/C is working overtime. One reviewer said that it cost $3 to make a gallon of water. I seriously doubt it is that much. If it was, my power bill would have gone up about $315 a month. It has been running about $50-$100 higher than normal, but again, I think it is probably due to all the record setting temps we have been having.

I was also concerned about all the internet hype and mis-information that is out there about drinking distilled water. We tried adding the minerals and himalayan salt to the water to put minerals back into it but we did not like the taste it added and the additional salt was unhealthy so now we just do plain distilled water. I think it it is more important to drink pure clean water and lots of it and to get your minerals from a healthy diet. It seems to me that most of the people claiming how distilled water is bad for you, because it takes out the minerals, are in the business of selling water filters instead of water distillers. In my opinion the distiller is a superior product over filtration and probably a less expensive alternative to water filters in the long run.

***UPDATE 09/12/12***
Shortly after writing my review above, a critical part on the distiller broke. There is a threaded post in the boiling chamber that serves as a water level indicator. You are supposed to fill the water up until it touches the bottom of the post. This post also acts as a stop for the lid mechanisim to not spin inside the tank when you are twisting the knob closed. Well the post broke off and was loose in the bottom of the chamber after making a batch of water. Further inspection shows that this post was either poorly welded or soldered into the boiling chamber. I sent an email first thing to customer service explaining my issue. By 4:30pm I had not received a response back and I called. I spoke to "Sue" who advised me she read my email but not had time to respond back. I explained to her the situation and she told me to ship the boiling chamber and post back to them. I would have to pay the shipping. She stated that my unit was outside the 90 day warranty and the repair would be free but that I would have to pay shipping. I have had the unit approximately 115 days. I expressed my displeasure in paying shipping for what is clearly a manufacturers defect and she replied that they do not make those in house and they are outsourced. I took this to mean "China" but do not know for certain that is what she meant. She further stated that since the boiling chamber is outsourced they do not have control over the quality of that part as if that gave some sort of justification as to why they would not pay for shipping on a defective item. Neverless, mine was outside of 90 days and if I wanted it repaired, I would have to pay the shipping. It was clear that I would not get anywhere. I boxed it up and sent it back and it cost me $17 in shipping to return the boiling chamber. I will be without use of the distiller for at least a week to ten days.
After spending $600 for this distiller, the fact that my father also purchased one, and I have told everyone about how great it is, and to have an issue such as this for what is clearly a manufacturer's defect it is very frustrating. I am then told they do not have quality control over this particular part. I promptly looked at my printed warranty and read the fine print which states it will be shipped back to me "freight collect." Not to mention we are now back to drinking tap water because of this issue. Yes, I am slightly pissed. The degree to how pissed I get is going to be determined by how quickly they repair it and ship it back to me. I also went back to the website to where I purchased the distiller and it clearly says "15 year warranty." None of the literature that came with unit says anything about a "90 day" warranty against defects. So it appears that the warranty against defects changes depending on what website you are on, what owner's manual receive, and who answers the phone in customer service. So pending the outcome of this, I am reducing my rating to three stars. I will post back when I receive the boiling chamber back. Hopefully, someone at PureWater will read this and do the right thing by reimbursing my shipping charges and not charging me shipping for what is clearly a defect on an "outsourced" part.

UPDATE 09/24/12
I recieved the boiling chamber back today. They did make the repair. However, the repair does not look very professional. It left a sharp edge that sticks out beyond the rim. I wonder how long it will take for my wife to cut her finger on it. I would guess the stem is soldered in place and not welded. The question in my mind is not if, but when it will break again. If it breaks again, I will not return it to Pure Water for repair. I will seek out a local company to either weld or re-solder it in place. There was no labor charge but they called asking for a credit card to charge me the return shipping. I protested and voiced my displeasure. I was told it would have to be approved by the boss before they would pay the return shipping to me. It took several days to get the boss's approval. When it was all said and done, it cost me $17 for shipping and two weeks without the use of my distiller. At no time did anyone say "We are sorry for the inconvenience."
I would have been totally satisfied had Pure Water asked for me to pay the shipping and upon inspection if they determined it was in fact a defect, they would have reimbursed me for the shipping and paid the return shipping. But instead I was called and asked to pay the shipping to send the unit back to me. Ultimately, they did pay the return shipping, but that isn't the point. Maybe I just expect too much after spending $600 for their distiller when other countertop distillers sell for $200.
Pure Water needs to pay attention to how some other companies stand behind their products. I could understand if I had the unit for several years and some part, like the fan, gave out. I would expect to have to either order a new fan and repair it myself or send the whole unit back for repair. However, I don't expect to have a hassle on something that was clearly defective. Also, Pure Water needs to clarify exactly what it's warranty is to consumers before they make their purchase. What I have seen says two years, another website says 15 years, and Pure Water themselves say 90 days when you call with a problem. If they would just offer a "No B.S. warranty" they would have a superior product. I am making my final grade at four stars and hoping for no more problems.

UPDATE 3/14/14
Still using it everyday. Wish I had another one.

UPDATE 5/8/2015
This thing runs all the time. We can't keep up with our demand and make 4-5 batches of water per day. I have found that every few months I am having to remove the screws and take the top cover off due to dust build up around the fins and the fan blades. It isn't difficult, I just remove the screws and use some compressed air to blow all the dust out. I do this outside so I don't spread the dust inside the house. I recently had a need to test some water for another project and I purchased a water tester here off Amazon that tells how many parts per million of solids are contained in the water. I tested some of the distilled water from this machine and it read "000" on the meter every time I tested. You just can't get cleaner water than that. I have a water filter that is factory installed in the refrigerator that filters the ice and water through the door. I tested that water and it consistently reads 27-29 parts per million. Water from the tap tests much higher and seems to change frequently on the quality according to the tester. So that tells me the $50 refrigerator filter works but just is not even close to the quality of the water from the distiller. I have already decided that if this unit ever quits, I will be buying a bigger one.

I bought 2 of them from Amazon 1 on April 7th 2014 and the other on June 4th 2014 because I was so impressed with the first one!

UPDATE October 2014: I use the Fenix HP25 every evening in the garden without any problems even when it is raining. Yesterday, this headlamp fell 2 METERS (6 FEET) onto solid garden tiles. The light was still on and I immediately check all of its spot and flood light functions and settings. RESULT: ABSOLUTELY NO PROBLEMS. This headlamp is therefore VERY ROBUST! LATEST UPDATE (31ST October 2014) I inadvertently did the same thing again while I was throwing water-dissolved fertilizer up a tree to my vanilla orchids! RESULTS: ABSOLUTELY NO PROBLEMS WITH ANY OF THE FUNCTIONS ON THE HEADLAMP, BUT I WILL NOW DEFINITELY TAKE IT OFF MY HEAD WHENEVER I DO THAT AGAIN!!

UPDATE FEBRUARY 2015: MY TWO FENIX HP25 HEADLAMPS HAVE WITHSTOOD OTHER DROPS ONTO SOLID TILES BY MYSELF AND OTHER PEOPLE, AS WELL AS BEING USED NEARLY EVERY DAY (WITH MULTIPLE DAILY BUTTON PRESSING AND LAMP TILTING) AND WEEKLY CHANGING OF THE RECHARGABLE AA BATTERIES.

UPDATE APRIL 22 2015
N.B. The new Eneloop 2,100 cycle BEAUTIFUL TROPICAL 4th generation AA battery Series are $ 23.45 for 8 which are only 50 cents more than the regular 8-pack of white Eneloop batteries!

[[ASIN:B00OV5FEI8 NEW Panasonic Eneloop 4th generation eneloop "Tropical Limited Edition" 8 Pack AA NiMH Pre-Charged Rechargeable Batteries]]

[[ASIN:B00JHKSN5I Panasonic BK-3MCCA8BA eneloop AA New 2100 Cycle Ni-MH Pre-Charged Rechargeable Batteries, 8 Pack]]

PREVIOUS IMPORTANT UPDATE
N.B. IMPORTANTLY, since Panasonic bought Eneloop from Sanyo they have placed a warning on their Panasonic Sanyo AA and AAA, as well as the Panasonic Eneloop Pro (previous Eneloop XX) batteries as `Do not use in water proof flashlight or any device with an air tight battery compartment'.

It is known that all batteries produce hydrogen (a flammable gas) particularly during overcharging and discharging but it appears that Panasonic are the first to announce this to cover themselves safety-wise. Thus, as with the risk of overcharging lithium ion batteries, the owners should be aware of the dangers of using AA/AAA batteries in water proof battery compartments on flashlights and headlamps. Delightfully, the Fenix HP25 battery compartment has a very long screw rod to completely shut it, and which I can leave slightly open (and still operate it) to vent any hydrogen when in use or when stored. If it is about to rain or is raining I then completely seal the battery compartment to prevent water entering it, and then re-open it in the dry. This gives me absolute peace of mind since I use this headlamp every day! This would however be much more difficult or impossible to do using the vast majority of water proof headlamps on the market today. Thus I think that all Fenix headlamps are uniquely easier to make safe due to their different design. THEREFORE, A GREAT HURRAY FOR FENIX!!

LATEST UPDATE AUGUST 2015: I HAVE REALLY FREQUENTLY USED AND ABUSED THESE TWO FENIX HP25 HEADLAMPS FOR OVER 16 MONTHS WITHOUT ANY ISSUES AND THEY REALLY ARE A FANTASTIC BUY!

Importantly, you can also very easily change the 4 AA batteries in total darkness due to the design of the battery case (large box with only one hand screw and the negative pole (helical) wires are easy to feel which makes you feel very secure should you be in a situation where you know the batteries are soon going to go!

Previous Review
I have had a number of headlamps (e.g. Petzl Tikka XP2, Silva Ninox) previously and they usually break very soon after use due to their cheap construction. The usual problem is that the lid of the battery compartment usually breaks thereby requiring you to subsequently seal it with strong tape. For a similar price, I opted to get the Fenix HP25 or HP15 which are both available in yellow or gray lamp colors.

[[ASIN:B00LGUAM2O Fenix Flashlights HP15 500-Lumen Headlamp]]

I settled for the HP25 since it had two lamps (one for wide (flood) beam and one for spot beam) rather than having one lamp with a diffuser. This headlamp has 180 lumens for each lamp, rather than having a 500 lumen beam on the HP15. top_rant.text:
Weed dragons. They say that weed dragons are myth, but believe me... they're not. Not in the slightest. So when I discovered my "little problems," as I like to call them, I had to turn to "Red Dragon VT 1-32 C 18,000 BTU Mini Weed Dragon Propane Vapor Torch Kit" to dispatch them. Sadly, despite having this wonderful tool at my disposal, my efforts apparently didn't turn out as intended, yet in all fairness, I must and should follow through with this review. First, a little background...

As stated before, weed dragons are no myth, but they are rare to catch a glimpse of. Most recent findings have noted that there are no less than four known sub-varieties of the common weed dragon. Though most are of the classification "mini sized" (about the size of a large morning star, with heavy chain and swarthy oak wooden handle attached, they can and do grow to be much larger. Like fish, these amazing entities adapt and grow to their surroundings, but for all intensive purposes it makes no difference to the narrative here; it's just provided for your information.... take it as you will, except for the two weed dragons in this situation were of medium size, similar to the size of your typical equine. As for their odd names, Weed Dragons are so called due to their affinity for the low lying grass lands. They are typically drawn to low level rock outcroppings, and if there are none close by, they take fancy to human structures... as in stone buildings, and I had the perfect place for them to show up. Did I mention that they were rare? Oh, I did... but I didn't mention that they're usually a nuisance when they do show up.

Now I've got this chunk of property that I use for my summer digs, moorish property, with an overlooking stone castle that has vast, green lands as far as the eye could see, and because I like my space (what Moorish dude doesn't?), I really don't take kindly random strangers visiting the property. But one night, after a mead laced solstice rumpus, these dragons just showed up and my life has never been the same since.

The first glimpse was really quite mesmerizing; brilliant, as I remember (... incidently, we Moorish dudes like to throw the word "brilliant" about alot). Imagine my joy and awe as I, for the first time in my life, caught sight of these two rare creatures. "Large sized weed dragons," I thought to myself, "... tis a rare sight to behold!." They were, and I state again, magnificent... nearly unbelievable so much as to rival my sighting of three wolves under a winter's moon, but I digress. So captivating, imagine that I had to SIT to finish my half full goblet of Tuscan Milk.

Ah, they WERE a sight to behold, but little did I know that by this chance showing, their first visit, innocuous... would eventually grow to something of a hindrance to life and livelihood. Over time, the pleasure of their visiting manners will shortly have turned into... but wait, I'm getting ahead of myself.

Infrequent shows of their company at first, a bit seemingly lost or shy, they, my little problems, quickly became accustomed to the place, making their presence known. Bolder each day, with the following being more bold than previous, yet even now I shudder to think of the development, how their transgressions turned my simple life into... but again, I'm getting ahead of myself.

Visits... oh yes, back to visits. Out of infrequent sightings came the blessings of quirky, infrequent visits. These infrequent visits blossomed into longer and longer stays; stretches of time where they would just... hang out. It was lovely. As time rolled on, with one season leading into the next, the visits became part of longer extended stays. Part joy and part permanence, these mixed bag stays, joyfully frequent bouquets of happenstance, ruminated in me distant musings for so many things that I had only years before pondered: relationships, occurrences past, times forgotten, times lost... all these swirled in my head.

Again, in the beginning, it was all very cute. Of what chance fortune did the initial glance and occasional flyby become something that of permanent stay or joy? Oh how I loved them. As imposing as they were (... what they WERE were fricken dragons after all, right?)... they were... quite beautiful; they didn't seem to bother anyone those two, so I didn't think twice when they moved into the coal shed.

After a while, when the fascination wore, I came to appreciate their practical presence; it became second nature to me to have them here in the moors, on my kick-ass moorish property, amongst the animals and peasants, living as they did in the coal shed, but I wasn't prepared for what they next had to offer! What was once whimiscal lounging and flitting about, these two became.... purposeful in action! They, in complete shock and amazement to me, began among other things, to toil, voluntarily, this being something that weed dragons just don't do. Well, I'm sure that at this point you could understand and empathize with the complete and unrestrained joy I was handed in discovering that these creatures, not only beautiful for what they were, also became useful. This came from no where... unprompted did the beauty disjointed by their purposeless visits blossom into something I truly could not fathom! In task, they lit the outside bread-kiln. They assisted the black smythe bellowing his fire for the shop, allowing for more effective heating the plowshares, swords, armor and maul. The peasants really loved those dragons, and in my heart, I guess did too. They were such an industrious lot, so it was really quite a shock to see things go south, but friends, I guess nothing lasts forever, does it? Though it saddens me to think about this, such a wretch am I for it, I must summons the strength to write on, so I shall...

I started noting "the change" one brisk autumn morning as the dog's food bowls were left dry. This incidently was accompanied by the fact that my dogs themselves, according to my peasants, were missing and had been for days. Discovering that my dogs too had indeed gone missing, the two events left me in a quandry. But then it got worse. I found my "little problems" one day in the chicken coop having their fill of the robust laying hens that had just matured this season. Days after, I noted my little problems to be lackey and immoblile upon the coal pile (What the frick were they just lounging about there doing nothing, I ask), and the horse stables... I won't even go on with what they did in the horse stables.

Pretty soon I found myself to be at wits end; I was low on coal, chickens, other random farm animals (to include the horses) AND peasants. No matter how I enjoyed the two, how much these dragons once brought me joy to no end, I eventually came to the conclusion that I had to do something, and I had to do that QUICKLY. So, guided by circumstance and hastened by fear of what was yet to come, having nothing else left for me to do, I placed my order to Amazon.com (online ordering is so rightous) for the "Red Dragon VT 1-32 C 18,000 BTU Mini Weed Dragon Propane Vapor Torch Kit."

Well, to get into the review, this "Red Dragon VT 1-32 C 18,000 BTU Mini Weed Dragon Propane Vapor Torch Kit" works pretty good... for fricken MINI dragons (thanks a lot, Flame Engineering), but it don't work so good for the larger variety weed dragon (Thanks, Flame Engineering!). Let me explain:

I may be a simple moor dweller, but I know improper product description labeling when I read it (really, shouldn't everyone?). Perhaps the folks over at Flame Engineering should make certain facts more apparent to the end consumer, like the fact that this torch isn't as all-purpose as they claim it to be. Sure, I get that it's specifically held to certain "small" tasks, but I can't get over the vague manner in which the product description is written (hello... there's such a thing as Technical Writing!). For example: I get that it's a pin-point torch, designed "for small jobs around the home & garden" but given the description, how's a guy to know it won't work for other stuff, stuff unlike mini dragons, or even dragons that aren't so big? Very deceptive. There are more than four subvarieties of Weed Dragons ya know! A suggestion: Hey, Flame Engineering, how about making something for us guys in the fricken MOORS! We've got all sorts of stuff out here requiring more than just a MINI torch approach, sheesh. As though it wasn't enough, you go on, as if to entice (or elude??? Which is it, Flame Engineering?!) by describing that the "Red Dragon VT 1-32 C 18,000 BTU Mini Weed Dragon Propane Vapor Torch Kit" is "...perfect for chemical free spot weeding, lighting charcoal, chimineas, campfires & "MORE."" There you go again. The choice of the word MORE implies a lot, doesn't it? VAGUE, VAGUE, VAGUE! Nice legal loophole for Flame Engineering, but helpful to the end consumer? I think not!

To rap this up, I used the "Red Dragon VT 1-32 C 18,000 BTU Mini Weed Dragon Propane Vapor Torch Kit" with steely intent to dispatch my two "little problems", but as you can guess by this point, the pinpoint 18,000 BTU Mini Weed Dragon Torch really didn't hold up against those two big-assed, loathsome dragons. Honestly, I fricken got cooked, as in almost died, Flame Engineering Dudes. After my ordeal I was left with a singed upper torso and blisters to my application hand (I'm right handed by the way, my fencing hand. Thanks, Flame Engineering for screwing me. No way to protect my castle against road pirates, years of training down the tubes).

So fricken pissed right now this Moorish dude is, but I gotta hand (I only have use of one hand, so I guess the use of the singular tense here is both ironic and appropriate) to them: this torch, easy to use, easy to setup and light... is fricken BOMB PROOF. Honestly, except for my third degree burns, loss of the use of my fencing hand, inability to protect the castle and partial melting of my chain-mail vest (I didn't mention that before, sorry....), the "Red Dragon VT 1-32 C 18,000 BTU Mini Weed Dragon Propane Vapor Torch Kit" really held up! Definitely worth the $0.00 I PAID FOR IT!! Yeah... suck it Flame Engineering... you heard it right: I got this honey for FREEEEEEEE! Count them... Zero duckets, Yo! Thanks to me having signed up for Amazon.com's Reward Visa, Flame Engineering didn't have the last laugh!

Overall, despite my basic complaint that marketing used a less than desireous product description here on Amazon, if I had a mini weed dragon lounging about (and use of my other hand...), I'm sure I could have readily dispatched it with this "Red Dragon VT 1-32 C 18,000 BTU Mini Weed Dragon Propane Vapor Torch Kit." So in the end, I must give credit where credit is due: Flame Engineering designed one kick as mini weed dragon dispatching torch.

So if you're in the market for a smallish propane torch to light various outdoor items, INCLUDING the asses of mini dragons to dispatch them away from your Moorish properties, and the use of both your hands, then this torch, by golly, is the torch for you!

[Update: While these two lard assed weed dragons are still trolling about the property, and while I spend most my indoor hours researching other ways to get rid of my dragon issues, I've come across the following to keep me motivated. I purchased and affixed to my stone wall this "Sword Dragon Killer - Wall Size Tribal Decal Vinyl Decor Graphics Wallpaper Sticker" (by Leon Online Box). This full wall sticker is dandy (Review pending) and keeps me motivated until such time I can either rid myself of these dragons, or purchase another item from Amazon.com (... they kick ass).

top_screaming.text: tHE DESCRIPTION OF THIS TROWEL IS VERY UNCLEAR AND AFTER READING THE COMMENTS, AND THOSE WHO HAVE SAID THEY HAVE DONE DRYWALL FOR 20 PLUS YEARS, I NOW KNOW WHY I SEE SO MANY STRUGGLE. i CAN'T EVEN WORD THE QUESTION PROPERLY TO GET THE ANSWER I NEED SO I FIGURED THIS MAY HELP MANY OTHERS. DRYWALL EDGES LENGTH WISE HAS A CONCAVE SECTION APPROX. 2" WIDE ON EVERY 4' PIECE. CALLED THE "FLATS". THE 4' WIDTH PART IS CALLED THE "BUTT" THESE ARE NOT CONCAVED. THEY ARE THE SAME THICKNESS AS THE VERY MIDDLE OF THE SHEET. a TRUE DRYWALL TROWEL HAS A CAMPHOR, LIKE A SNOW SKI. IF YOU SET IT ON A FLAT SERVIDE LIKE A COUNTER TOP, DEPENDING ON THE LENGTH OF THE TROWEL YOU CAN SLIDE A PIECE OF CARDBOARD, OR SAY 2 QUARTERS STACKED ON EACH OTHER, UNDER THE VERY MIDDLE OF THE TROWEL. THIS IS FOR THE FLATS, AND BUTT JOINTS. TAPE WITH A 6"KNIFE, NOT A TROWEL. TROWELS COME IN DIFFERENT LENGTHS. 10" , 10.5" 12" , 14", & 16". i LOVE MY 16". THE MIDDLE SIZE TROWELS ARE USED FOR THE 2ND COAT. CONSTANT PRESSURE IN, PLENTY OF MUD, NUMEROUS PASSES UNTIL A VERY SMOOTH FINISH. wORKING THE AIR BUBBLES OR DRY COMPOUND ETC. DRY OVER NIGHT.  IF YOU USE THE RIGHT AMOUNT OF MUD, YOU WILL LEAVE SOME BEHIND AT BOTH ENDS OF THE TROWEL. NOT EVERYTIME, WHEN YOU GET THE AMOUNT JUST RIGHT. IT DOESN'T MATTER, USE YOUR 6" KNIFE AND CLEAN IT UP. PUT MORE PRESSURE AWAY FROM THE MIDDLE OF THE SEAM, AND YOU WILL REMOVE ANY RIDGE.  I USE THE 12" TROWEL FOR THIS AND THE 10.5 FOR MY OUTSIDE CORNERS. THIRD COAT, EITHER THE 14 OR 16" TROWEL. REPEAT THE SAME PROCESS. PRESSURE IN MAKING NUMEROUS PASSES TO SMOOTH OUT. REMOVE ANY COMPOUND THAT SNEAKS OUT AT THE ENDS OF THE TROWEL. NOW THE BUTTS. YOU HAVE 2 PIECES THE SMAE THICKNESS BUTTING EACH OTHER AND YOUR ADDING DRYWALL COMPOUND AND DON'T WANT TO SEE ANY BUILT UP LUMP. FIRST TRY AND AVOID TOTAL BUTTS WHEN HANGING. MAKE YOUR SEAMS FALL OVER DOORS OR WINDOWS IN THE MIDDLE. BEST NOT TO BREAK AT THE CORNERS. TRIMMING, YOU WILL HAVE A LARGER THAN NORMAL WALL THICKNESS, RIGHT AT THE POINT WHERE YOUR TRIM BUTTS EACH OTHER. WHEN YOU HAVE A FULL 4' BUTT, I USE MY 12" TROWEL AND DO MY 2ND COAT RIGHT AFTER i TAPE IT. NO WAIT. DON'TAPPLY AS MUCH PRESSURE. uSE YOUR 6" KNIFE TO ADD A LITTLE TO THE OUTSIDES OF THE MUD. ON THE 3RD COAT, I USE MY 14 OR 16" AND HIT BOTH SIDES OF THE BUTT SEAM, MIDDLE OUT. NOW I'M OUT 32" REMOVE RIDGES ON THE OUTER AREAS. BECAUSE THERE WAS MUD APPLIED WHEN YOU TAPED, THE 3RD COAT WILL DRY SOONER PULLING THE WATER INTO THE DRY COMPOUND. I THEN USR MY 16" DOWN THE MIDDLE OF THE BUTT WITH A FAIR OUT OF PRESSURE, AND MY 6" TO FEATHER THAT INTO WHAT I HAVE THERE FROM THE NED COAT. REMEMBER I MADE 2 PASSES ONE ON EACH SIDE. NOW I'M ABOUT 32 " WIDE, AND FEATHERED OUT. THE LAST COAT THE I ALREADY STATED GOES RIGHT UP THE MIDDLE OF THE  BUTT. THE MUD GOES ANOTHER AO" OR SO BEYOND THAT ON BOTH SIDES. USE YOUR 6" KNIFE AGAIN LIKE YOUR SKIM COATING A WALL, AT THE EDGES OF YOUR OAST PAST WITH 16" TROWEL AND AT THE OUTSIDE OF THE DOUBLE PASS YOU MADE ON THE SECOND COAT. SO NOW YOUR BUTT JOINT IS ABOUT 3 FT. WIDE, AND FEATHERED TO A FLAT SURFACE. YOU CAN REMOVE SMALL RIDGES WITH YOUR KNIFE, AND SKIM ANY AREA'S YOU FEEL NEED TOUCHING UP. YOU CANNOT DO THIS WITH A PLASTERING TROWEL. YOU CAN, BUT DOING A WHOLE HOUSE WITH THE WRONG TROWELS IS A NIGHTMARE. PLASTERING TROWELS ACTUALLY WITH HAVE A SMALL UPWARD TURN SO IT DOESN'T DIG INTO THE PLASTER OR COMPOUND. THE DRYWALL ROWEL IS SHAPED LIKE A C, EXAGGERATION YES. SO THE ITEM DESCRIPTION FOR THIS TROWEL IS NOT CLEAR. TWO DIFFERENT TOOLS COMPLETELY. oFTEN WHEN I'M DOING ANY PLASTERING OR SKIP TROWELING A CEILING I USE A POOL TROWEL. THE ENDS OF THE TROWEL ARE ROUND OR OVAL. THE SHAPE OF THE TOWEL IS FLAT OR EVEN SLIGHTLY TURNED AWAY FROM THE SURFACE YOUR WORKING ON. THE POOL TROWEL ALSO COMES IN A 10 AND 14". MY DAYS  PUSHING ON A 14" TO SKIP TROWEL A CEILING ARE IN THE REARVIEW. i USE THE LARGER WITH 2 HANDS IF I'M REALLY WORKING A PLASTER WALL AND WANT IT LIKE GLASS. SPRAYING WITH WATER AND REALLY RUBBING IT OUT, SAME AS FINISHING CRETE. jUST ON THE WALL. I WOULD BE HESITANT TO BUY THESE TROWELS OR ANY ONLINE. KIND OF LIKE A HAMMER OR HOCKEY STICK, IT HAS TO FEEL RIGHT. TRYING TO PLASTER WITH A DRYWALL TROWEL WILL ALWAYS BE A FIGHT.  A BIG WASTE OF TIME. LOOK DOWN THE TROWEL IF IT'S BOWED UP TOWARDS THE HANDLE IT'S FOR DRYWALL. IT ITS FLAT ITS FOR PLASTERING. A 12" FLAT PLASTERING TROWEL WORKS GREAT FOR SKIP TROWELING. THESE ARE NOT 100W EQUIVALENT LIGHTS. THESE ARE 15W UNITS. SO, EACH UNIT IS BETWEEN 30W AND 40W EQUIVALENT. SO, THE TWO OF THEM COMBINED IS NO MORE THAN 60W TO 80W EQUIVALENT, MAX.

AND I WOULD NOT RECOMMEND THEM FOR OUTDOOR USE AS THESE UNITS ARE FAIRLY DELICATE. AND I EXPECT IT WOULDN'T TAKE VERY MUCH RAIN AT ALL TO DESTROY THEM.

IT WOULD REQUIRE FOUR OF THESE UNITS, AIMED AT THE WHITE CEILING, TO LIGHT UP MY 400 SQUARE FOOT LIVING ROOM SUFFICIENTLY. BUT ONE OR TWO OF THEM, AIMED AT A WHITE CEILING, SHOULD BE ENOUGH FOR A SMALL TO MEDIUM SIZED BEDROOM. THIS ALSO SHOULD INDICATE THAT THEY PROBABLY WOULDN'T BE USEFUL FOR AN OUTDOOR AREA THAT IS MUCH BIGGER THAN YOUR FRONT PORCH.

THESE UNITS ARE ONLY GOOD FOR ABOUT TWO OR THREE YEARS BEFORE THEY BEGIN TO LOSE BRIGHTNESS AND REQUIRE REPLACEMENT.
THAT IS, IF THE INTERNAL ELECTRONICS DON'T SUDDENLY FAIL WITHIN SIX MONTHS, RENDERING THE UNIT INTO AN EXPENSIVE PAPERWEIGHT. AS DID ONE OF THE FOUR THAT I HAD PURCHASED OVER A PERIOD OF TWO YEARS.

ON THE LIGHTER SIDE, (PUN) THE COLORS ARE RICH AND OFFER MANY VARIATION CHOICES AND THE TONES LOOK CONSISTENT ACROSS UNITS.
AND THESE UNITS ARE VERY SMALL AND THEY GET BARELY WARM TO THE TOUCH DUE TO THE LOW WATTAGE; SO, THEY CAN BE PLACED ON JUST ABOUT ANY HARD SURFACE WITHOUT FEAR OF HEAT DAMAGE OR TAKING UP A LOT OF SPACE.
AND THE PROGRAMMABILITY OF THE WHITE LIGHT BETWEEN COOL AND WARM COLOR TEMPERATURES IS A NICE OPTION.
THAT MEANS THAT THIS IS AN RGB-CCT LIGHT AND THERE REALLY ARE NOT MANY RGB-CCT LIGHTS OUT THERE TO BE HAD. MOST OF THE LIGHTS THAT YOU WILL FIND ARE ONLY RGB-W. WHICH MEANS THE WHITE LIGHT DOES ONLY ONE COLOR TEMPERATURE, WHICH IS COOL WHITE. SO, DO NOT FORGET TO MAKE THAT DISTINCTION IF YOU LIKE THE IDEA OF PROGRAMMING THE COLOR TEMPERATURE OF YOUR WHITE LIGHT BETWEEN WARM WHITE AND COOL WHITE. BECAUSE THIS IS ONE OF THE FEW UNITS ON AMAZON THAT IS CAPABLE OF THIS.
ALSO THE PROGRAMMABLE COLOR FLASH IS COOL. ALTHOUGH IT WOULD BE COOLER TO HAVE A PROGRAMMABLE COLOR FADE OPTION.
ALSO, WITH FRESH BATTERIES, THE IR REMOTE CONTROL IS BRIGHT ENOUGH TO REFLECT OFF THE WALL TO THE RECEIVER. SO, THAT MEANS THAT YOU DON'T NECESSARILY NEED TO HAVE THE UNITS POINTED DIRECTLY AT YOU IN ORDER TO CONTROL THEM WITH THE REMOTE. THE LIGHTS CAN BE AIMED PRETTY MUCH TOWARDS THE WALL AND STILL RECEIVE COMMANDS FROM THE REMOTE CONTROL IF THE REMOTE IS AIMED AT THE WALL AND THE IR LIGHT IS REFLECTED TO THE FRONT OF THE UNIT. BUT KEEP IN MIND THAT FRESH BATTERIES IN THE REMOTE IS ESSENTIAL FOR THIS TECHNIQUE TO WORK.
AND IDEALLY, YOU REALLY WANT THESE LIGHTS TO BE POINTED AT A WALL OR CEILING BECAUSE IT SPREADS AND DIFFUSES THE LIGHTING AND IT PREVENTS YOU FROM LOOKING INTO THE EMITTERS. AND YOU NEVER WANT TO LOOK DIRECTLY INTO THE EMITTERS IF IT CAN BE AVOIDED, THAT IS NOT A PLEASANT THING TO DO, EVEN WITH LEDS THAT HAVE THIS LOW OF AN OUTPUT.

ALSO THE PRICE OF THESE UNITS HAS DOUBLED SINCE THE LAST TIME THAT I PURCHASED THEM WHEN THEY WERE ONLY FIFTEEN U.S. DOLLARS EACH. SO, I WILL NOT BE BUYING ANY MORE OF THEM.

SO, I HAVE SINCE OPTED FOR AN ACTUAL 100W EQUIVALENT UNIT FOR MY LIVING ROOM THAT IS ONLY RGB-W AND NOT RGB-CCT LIKE THESE LIGHTS ARE.
THE NEW UNIT WAS SIXTY-FIVE U.S.DOLLARS BUT THE TRADEOFF IN THE VOLUME OF LIGHT IS MORE THAN WORTH IT AS THE SINGLE 100W EQUIVALENT RGB-W IS MORE THAN SUFFICIENT FOR LIGHTING MY 400 SQUARE FOOT LIVING ROOM. AND IT IS ABOUT FOUR OR FIVE TIMES THE PHYSICAL SIZE OF THESE UNITS SO, IT ALSO BARELY GETS WARM TO THE TOUCH AT ALL, DUE TO IT'S LARGER SIZE, PRESUMABLY. AND I HARDLY MISS THE WARM WHITE CAPABILITY OF THE SMALL LIGHTS AT ALL. THE NEW UNIT ALSO CAME WITH A RF REMOTE INSTEAD OF IR AND CAN BE CONTROLLED VIA SMARTPHONE, ALLEGEDLY. ALTHOUGH I HAVE NO INTENTION OF TRYING THAT.

SO, THERE IS MY ESSAY ON THESE LITTLE LIGHTS. I HOPE THIS WAS HELPFUL TO YOU.

I'LL TRY AND CONTACT THE MANUFACTURER OR PERHAPS AMAZON TO SEE IF I CAN REPLACE THE PIECE I'VE GOT WITH SOMETHING THAT WAS PROPERLY MADE WITH SOME ACTUAL QUALITY CONTROL. I'LL UPDATE LATER TO LET EVERYONE KNOW WHAT TRANSPIRED.  4-2-2020 UPDATE: AMAZON VERY KINDLY ACCEPTED THE FIRST "DRILLMATE" BACK AND SENT ME ANOTHER. THE SECOND COPY WAS JUST AS BAD AS THE FIRST ONE WITH IDENTICALLY THE SAME PROBLEMS AS DESCRIBED IN MY EARLIER EVALUATION. I AM OLD ENOUGH TO REMEMBER THE DAYS WHEN TOOLS OF ALL SORTS MADE IN THE UNITED STATES WERE OF THE HIGHEST QUALITY OR THEY WOULD NOT HAVE STAYED ON THE MARKET. THE "CRAFTSMAN" BRAND, MARKETED BY THE NOW DEFUNCT SEARS-ROEBUCK, IS/WAS A GOOD EXAMPLE. I HAVE CRAFTSMAN TOOLS DATING BACK 50 YEARS THAT REMAIN AS ROBUST AND PERFECTLY USABLE AS WHEN I BOUGHT THEM. MOST OF THOSE TOOLS IN THOSE BYGONE DAYS WERE MADE IN THE U.S., WHILE SOME OTHERS CAME OUT OF THE UK OR GERMANY OR SOMEWHERE IN WESTERN EUROPE. ONE COULD HAVE CONFIDENCE THAT THESE TOOLS WERE OF THE HIGHEST QUALITY.  THE SAME CANNOT BE SAID FOR TOOLS COMING OUT OF THE PRC. THE BUYER MUST GENERALLY ASSUME THAT ANYTHING MADE IN THE PEOPLE'S REPUBLIC IS SUB-STANDARD UNTIL PROVEN OTHERWISE. THIS "MILESCRAFT" PIECE OF JUNK IS A GOOD EXAMPLE. ANY CRAFTSMAN MUST RUE THE DAY THAT THEIR VARIOUS AND SUNDRY GOVERNMENTS ALLOWED SO MANY ENGINEERING AND MANUFACTURING SKILLS , THROUGH THE URGING AND MACHINATIONS OF CORPORATE GREED,  TO BE GIVEN OVER TO THE PRC. BUYER BEWARE.

YOU MUST HAVE WI FI AND PUBLIC WI FI WILL NOT WORK. AFTER CHARGING MAGIC JACK PLUS TO MY VISA CREDIT CARD, I MADE SEVERAL ATTEMPTS TO RETURN THE MAGIC JACK THROUGH THEIR WEBSITE. THE FORMAT TO OBTAIN AN RMA (return merchandise authorization) REQUIRED ME TO ENTER FOUR ITEMS: MY EMAIL ADDRESS, TRACKING NUMBER, ORDER NUMBER AND MY CREDIT CARD NUMBER. AFTER ENTERING THE REQUIRED NUMBERS CORRECTLY I WAS DENIED AN RMA WITH THIS MESSAGE AFTER SEVERAL ATTEMPTS, "WE CANNOT FIND ANY MAGIC JACK ORDERS THAT MATCH THE INFORMATION PROVIDED THAT ARE WITHIN THE 30 DAY TRIAL PERIOD." A CHAT WITH ABRAHAM AND THREE OTHER ONLINE CHAT STAFFERS REPEATEDLY SUGGESTED THAT I RETRY SUBMITTING THE FORMAT OVER AND OVER. MAGIC JACK STALLS EVERYTHING TO REACH THE END OF THE THIRTY DAY TRIAL PERIOD WHILE REFUSING TO PROVIDE THE REQUIRED RMA NUMBER FOR RETURNING THE MAGIC JACK. THIS IS SHABBY AND TACKY. THEN THEIR "POLICY" PREVENTS A RETURN AFTER THEY HAVE SUCCESSFULLY STALLED THE CUSTOMER BEYOND THE 30 DAY TRIAL.

THE CHARGE OF $4.26 WAS POSTED VERY EARLY AFTER MY INITIAL COMPLAINT AND JUST THREE DAYS AFTER THE ORDER WAS PLACED. THE BALANCE OF $63.49 WAS POSTED TO MY CREDIT CARD ACCOUNT JUST TWO DAYS AFTER THE END OF THE THIRTY DAY TRIAL PERIOD. AN ATTEMPT TO REVERSE 100% OF THESE CHARGES WITH VISA WAS DENIED BECAUSE MAGIC JACK EXPLAINED TO THE VISA DISPUTE REPRESENTATIVE THAT "MAGIC JACK POLICY REQUIRED THE MAGIC JACK TO BE RETURNED DURING THE 30 DAY TRIAL PERIOD." VISA EXPLAINED TO MAGIC JACK THAT I HAD EMAILED MY DESIRE TO RETURN THE MAGIC JACK EARLY DURING THE THIRTY DAY TRIAL PERIOD. VISA EXPLAINED TO ME THAT MAGIC JACK COULD NOT REVERSE ANY OF THE CHARGES BECAUSE OF MAGIC JACK'S POLICY! I HAD ONLY $33.00 OF THE TOTAL CHARGES REVERSED WITH A CREDIT TO MY CREDIT CARD.

FURTHER, SOME OF THE REVIEWS SHOCKINGLY CLAIM THAT THE TELEPHONE NUMBER PROVIDED IN THEIR AREA CODE WAS A NUMBER THAT REQUIRED THE CUSTOMERS OWN COMMUNITY AND NEIGHBORS TO CALL LONG DISTANCE! EXTREME PROBLEMS ARE REVEALED ABOUT MAGIC JACK!

RUN FROM MAGIC JACK AND YOU WILL BE THANKFUL THAT YOU DID. READ THE NEGATIVE REVIEWS AS THE GREAT REVIEWS ARE PROBABLY BY MAGIC JACK'S OWN PEOPLE! I ALWAYS THOUGHT MAGIC JACK WAS A REPUTABLE COMPANY AND NOW I AM CONVINCED THAT THEY ARE PLAYING A SHABBY GAME OF DECEIT WITH THEIR CUSTOMERS.

top_screaming.text: WOW COULD NOT BE HAPPIER,  USED THESE ON A HITCH JUST A FEW DAYS AFTER RECEIVING THIS ITEM.  DRILLED A HOLE VERY EASY, THEN WHEN IT GOT TO THE ACTUAL TAPPING PART OF THE DRILL THAT TOO WENT IN VERY EASY, STOPPED THE DRILL ONCE THE THREADS WERE ON THE HOLE. DID NOT AND HAVE USED THE DEBUR PART, HAVE BEEN AFRAID OF MAKING IT COME BACK ACROSS THE THREAD MAKING SECTION AND MAYBE RUINING THE THREADS CREATED ON THE SIDES OF THE HOLE. HAVE DRILLED INTO THE HANDLE BARS OF A BIKE SIX HOLES, ALL LIKE A CHARM,  HAVE DRILLED INTO THE BIKE FRAME TUBING FOUR TIMES AND THAT TOO WENT LIKE A CHARM. LIKE MOST PEOPLE THAT ARE FIX IT GUYS, I HAVE A PARTS BIN HOLDER ON THE WALL WITH SCREWS THAT ARE SORTED BY HEAD TYPE, SO I DRILLED THE SIX SIZES THAT THIS SET MAKES INTO AN ALUMINUM PLATES THAT IS 1/4 INCH THINK NOW WHEN A NEED TO DRILL A HOLE TO ADD A BOLT TO HOLD SOMETHING GOT TO THE METAL I JUST DUMP THE BOLTS ONTO A NEWSPAPER AND FIND A BOLT FROM THE PILE THAT FITS SNUGLY INTO ONE OF THE HOLES ON THE ALUMINUM PLATE THAT HAS ALL SIX THREADED HOLES ON IT.  ALSO CUT THE PLATE TO FIT INTO THE CASE ON THE LEFT SIDE WHERE THE QUICK RELEASE IS HELD.  TO ENABLE ME TO TAKE THE BOLT FINDER PLATE THAT WAS MADE WITH THE BITS AS I GRAB THEM AND GO FROM THE STORAGE ROOM TO THE WORK SITE.  I RECOMMEND AND DID USE THE  CRC SL2512 Soluble Oil, 32 Fl Oz  DRILLING CUTTING FLUID THAT I ALSO BOUGHT HERE ON AMAZON FOR KEEPING THE BIT COOL AND LUBRICATED WHEN DRILLING AND TAPPING WITH THIS SET AND THAT TOO WORKS LIKE IT SHOULD.  JUST OPEN THE LID PLACE IT ON THE TABLE OR GROUND AND THEN STICK A PENCIL OR PEN INSIDE THE OPENING OF THE BOTTLE TO GET SOME LIQUID ON IT - THEN LET THE LIQUID DROP OFF THE PEN OR PENCIL ONTO THE METAL THAT YOU ARE DRILLING INTO.  DRILL ABOUT 10 TO 15 SECONDS THEN ADD A FEW MORE DROPS, AND SO ON UNTIL HOLE IS DONE,  THIS PRODUCTS WORKS, JUST BE CAREFUL TO NOT STRIP THE THREADS IN THE HOLE YOU JUST CREATED WHEN PUTTING THE DRILL INTO REVERSE TO GET THE BIT OUT OF THE HOLE.  YES I DO RECOMMEND THIS IF YOU NEED TO MAKE IS METAL WITH THREAD FOR ADDING BOLTS TO HOLD THINGS ONTO THE METAL, AS LONG AS YOU HAVE CUTTING / DRILLING FLUID AS WELL, THESE REALLY WORK AND ARE EASY TO USE, AND WEAR EYES PROTECTION AND GLOVES IF YOU PLAN ON TOUCHING THE METAL SHAVINGS DRILLED OUT OF THE HOLES. A PAPER TOWEL TO WIPE THE SHAVING OUT OF THE WAY AFTER DRILLING IS DONE IS ALSO HELPFUL, YOU CAN DAB THEM UP THE SHAVINGS AND DROP THE PAPER-TOWEL RIGHT INTO THE TRASH CAN MOST OF THE TIME,

top_five_star_obscene.text:
***Update*** This Bag FLOATS with all your tools in it
No its not waterproof. Yes your laptop will get wet, along with anything else that is in the bag. But HOLY SHIT IT FLOATS. I discovered this quite by accident. I was working on a single post oil platform, that means climbing up a ladder with the stupid cage around it. Well its damn near impossible to accomplish this while wearing a type IV coast guard rated PFD, AND this backpack. So I left it on the boat and we climbed up. We were using a piece of 3/8th inch manila grass rope to pull our tool bags up to the top deck with when the rope snapped on my bag, and it went right in the drink. My bag weighs exactly 28 lbs. I know this because I weigh it often to get on helicopters. All 28 lbs of my bag, tools and all, just sat there floating, not quite up out of the water but not sinking either. The boat re-positioned, and the deck hand used the hook to grab it out of the water and while my company laptop is toast, along with a $1000 fluke process meter, I also saved close to $1000 worth of tools.

Ive carried this bag now for almost 6 moths. the left shoulder strap stitching is showing signs of strain but it keeps holding, and some of the nylon is totally stretched. the stitching for the top carry handle came apart but the handle is still there. but the zippers, the buckles, and all the straps are still together and still holding super well.

While this bag is not my "total ultimate everything possible package" It IS a tough little booger and the damn thing floats.
*********************************************************

My girl got this for me as a birthday gift, after listening to me complain about the bag that I was provided by my employer. I work as a controls technician for an offshore oil and gas producer. I often have to go into the field by boat, swing from the boat onto a platform, and then climb 6 or 7 flights of stairs to get up to the production deck. I need a bag that is tough, somewhat weather resistant, can carry my laptop, spare battery, all manner of connecting cables and some basic electrical and pneumatic instrument tools, meter, as well as my lunch, any specialty tools, and spare parts. This bag fits the bill perfectly. Ive only carried it for about a week now and I am pretty much in love with it. For starters its light. Way lighter than the pelican U100 Elite Urban bag that I was carrying. It SEEMS tough enough to hold up for a good while, but only time will tell. It fits my laptop quite snugly, which is actually a good thing. My bag often gets thrown to me or to the boat deckhand in heavy seas when just a hand off isnt really possible. Its also often chucked into the baggage area of a helicopter and often other peoples bags are thrown in on top of it.

The tools that I carry every day are 10 in crescent, 6 in crescent, 6 in knipex pliers, 10 in knipex pliers, 1/4 in Swagelok tubing benders, 9/16th in wrench, 7/16th in wrench, Super Mini Deluxe ratchet set, RJ 45 connector crimps, a bunch of terminal board screwdrivers, 3 big flat tip screwdrivers, #2 Phillips screwdriver, Wire strippers, electricians scissors, fluke 789 processmeter, PTFE and Electrical tape, bottle of STL-8. I also carry 2 external Hard Drives, A couple of jump drives, Gloves, Safety Glasses, Ear Plugs, Extra Socks, A pack of baby wipes, Laptop charger. The front clip pouch is perfect to hold all of my coiled up Serial cables, PLC connector cables, HMI connector cables, Ethernet cables, specialty meter leads, and about 20 feet of 16 ga wire. I keep all of my ferrules, wirenuts, terminal connectors and other misc crap and a small set of 1/4 in drive sockets in zipper pouches. I realize this is NOT a huge list of tools for anyone that works with tools. But being a controls guy this is just about all that I need.

So for my complaints, The bag is very snug on my 15 in laptop. If my PC was any bigger I wouldnt be able to carry this bag. The laptop protection is minimal, the slot for the laptop is suspended off the bottom of the bag keeping some airspace between it and the bottom, so that your computer doesnt get smacked when you set it down. And also creating a convenient space for parts to wedge themselves and get lost as well as defeating the purpose of the suspension. When a laptop is in the pouch, especially a 15 inch the corners of the PC stretch the material at the very top of the bag, and the only protection for the PC in that region is just the canvas covering it, This is a bit worrying to someone like me that admittedly literally tosses my bag across a sometimes 6 to 10 foot gap between platform and work boat. I think that I may end up gluing some foam in the bottom right under the laptop pouch and cutting the bottom open, then gluing a strip of foam in the top of the bag right under the handle to protect the corners that stick up. This will also serve to block off that hiding space that electrical connectors seem to be fond of rolling into.

All in all it seems like a really nice purchase and seems quite sturdy. Giving 4 stars for the minimal complaints listed, If there are any quality issues I will certainly update this review.
top_five_star_obscene.title: This bag made the Electrician jealous, and that's not even the best part.

top_five_star_obscene.text: This is a very quiet and powerful garbage disposal.  For those who had problems with it being too noisy or shaky they probably installed it wrong.  One thing the instructions say, but not well is about the rubber ring that goes on the bottom.  The first time I installed the garbage disposal I pushed the ring all the way up until the bottom was flush with the housing.  When I turned it on, it was noisy and shaky like most of the complaints talk about.  When I looked at the instructions again I realized that there is a groove the rubber ring goes up to, and there is supposed to be a lip of rubber ring below the housing.  Also, you have to make sure to fully seat the garbage disposal when you are attaching it to the sink.  I had to get a screw driver to help give me some torque to make sure it was fully screwed on.  All in all putting it together, taking it apart, and putting it back together took me an hour or so.  It took me longer to take the old piece of crap that was there out.
top_five_star_obscene.title: Quiet and Will Eat Through Anything

top_five_star_obscene.text:
So first of all, the design and quality of this shower head is better than any crappy water pik I've ever owned (I have 2 broken right now). The water picks rely on a "pressed" head that leaks if you bump it in the slightest, they're total crap. This shower head is solid, isn't "pressed" together like the water piks (stupid design considering the water pressure is constantly pushing it apart) and the hose is solid metal, not 1/2 material hoses like the water piks (they're plastic & metal). This head had outlasted 2 other heads and that's why I'M BUYING ANOTHER. To the GENIUSES commenting about water pressure, the water pressure comes from the source, not the shower head, duh. Also if your shower head is "stuck" on one color that's because you're water pressure sucks. It needs pressure to 1) light up 2) cycle through the colors hence NO BATTERIES. Great length on the hose and the piece that holds the head is solid, can't see it breaking easily.
Only "down side" is that there are almost 3 spray settings, 2 + a blend of the two. 1) wide/rain type and 2) a 3 stand stream meant for high pressure, but that's all I need.
Too many fools writing bad reviews on good products, then idiots writing 5 star reviews on an item they got the day they are writing the review. They should pay me to review products lol, I keep it real and I'm thorough. Happy showering!

top_five_star_obscene.text:
Excellent solution for collecting chips from planing lumber. To use this system, I purchased a plastic trash can from a local hardware store along with large plastic bags. I insert a plastic bag in the trash can and attached the Dewalt DW 7353 Chip Collection System to the trash can, then to my Dewalt DW 7353 Planer.

When the DW735 Planer is in use, it transfers the chips to the can. Clean up is relatively easy, by removing the Chip Collection bag from trash can and removing the plastic bag full of chips.

The two disadvantages of the system are (1) chip buildup between the rim of the trash can and where the accessory bag attaches to the can and (2) occasionally the accessory bag will break away from the trash can which causes chips to fall on the floor.

Overall, I recommend this system even with the two disadvantages because it save hours of cleanup and it is the cheapest solution to chip collection that I have found.
top_five_star_obscene.title: Dewalt DW 7353 Chip collection Accessory

top_five_star_obscene.text:
Watching “Chicago Fire” and all those scenes involving the use of a Halligan Bar left me wanting something similar at home … a powerful, sturdy tool of decent length to pry, hammer and pound. While purchasing an actual Halligan Bar was impractical and rather expensive; purchasing the STANLEY FATMAX XTREME FUBAR III was not only a viable compromise, but a worthy investment.

WHAT THE FUBAR III OFFERS

This is a multi-faceted tool. A 30 inch one-piece forged steel bar that is beveled and split on one end with the other end serving as a hammer and board grabber. The shaft between the two “business” ends of this tool is squared, with defined edges that offer a solid gripping surface.
One of the biggest attributes of this tool is the simple, but effective design. The almost yard-long length provided ample leverage for most any general demolition or prying purpose. Being a single-piece tool, there is no worry of heavy use deteriorating the tool and causing parts to dangerously separate (like a sledgehammer head coming off the handle). The rather even distribution of the bar’s weight makes this extremely easy to wield.
The “striking” end of the FUBAR III is hammer-like with a flat striking-head that protrudes from one side and an “F-shaped” jaw with teeth protruding from the other side. The hammer head is sizeable enough to bust 2x4s with relative ease and the grabbing sized is great for grabbing onto the same-sized boards to bend and break. The prying end has a graduated thickness for much better prying leverage than flat prying bars.

While the ends of the FUBAR III are certainly effective in both design and function, the shaft is what makes this tool work so well. Aside from somewhat squared shape of the handle, the shaft is recessed on both sides to accommodate textured rubber grips to be bolted into the tool, creating a flush surface grip rather than simply being attached on top. The grips being incorporated into the bar rather than on the bar enhance the one-piece feel to the tool. The end result is a solid, sturdy grip that is critical when using the bar.

While my main use for the FUBAR III is relegated to prying jobs, the striking and grabbing end will allow me to take down the backyard deck rather than pay someone else to do it … I’m looking forward to putting the FUBAR III to full use. While my initial purchase of this bar was simply a whim, I’ve found it to be a handy tool to keep in the back of my SUV … great tool for camping: hammering tent pegs, breaking down wood for a fire and even a convenient personal defense tool.
top_five_star_obscene.title: A Versatile Tool of Destruction …

top_five_star_obscene.text:
Loving this filter media so far. I have allergies and own an old house that seems to have supernatural dust generating capabilities. Not to mention a cat and a filthy-ass dog :P So I made much better filters for my window air conditioners than the crappy screens they come with these days. Still have the screens in there but have this stuff cut to fit as a pre-filter I guess you would call it. Works beautifully.

And made a nice pre-filter for my dehumidifier in the basement. I would highly recommend it for that. I am on my 3rd dehumidifier and they get really cruddy inside with the stock filter in a dusty basement. And yes, I clean the screen filters in my A/Cs and the dehumidifier regularly, but that doesn't change the fact that the sorry-excuse-for-a-filter screen material lets dust through. I started with the dehumidifier I have now (when it was new) using a slightly thinner fiberglass filter media for a pre-filter and the coils and everything else inside look spotless like brand new. Can only imagine this thicker polyester stuff will be even better as far as filtration and longevity. And I never liked the idea of fiberglass as filter media as fiberglass particles are no good to breathe.

I even wrapped a piece around the bottom (air inlet) of my tower style Oreck XL. They have the ionizing collector cell but once again just a substandard crappy regular screen for a pre-filter. I no longer hear the cracking of dust particles occasionally getting sucked into it. And the collector cell can preserve itself for more fine particulates and pollutants.

I know this may seem a little extreme to some people but after seeing the inside of these devices from a few years with stock filters it makes nothing but sense to me. I don't want an air conditioner or a dehumidifier that gets all scummy on the inside and eventually starts growing mold and bacteria. And aside from the vastly better indoor air quality... all that crud, reduced airflow, and improper warming of the coils makes them run less efficiently, can cause them to not drain properly, and even freeze up and destroy themselves. And new equipment costs me money!

If only the scum bags responsible for manufacturing all of this sub standard, disposable, planned obsolescence trash would use filters like this to begin with it would seriously be a better world.

So after tons of research I found this MERV 8 polyester stuff seemed to be the best thing going for these types of applications. And after receiving it I am very satisfied. I imagine the way it's made some slight variation in thickness or look of the polyester is normal, but quality is fairly consistent across the media. And really for the price you can't beat it. This roll should last me quite a few years. And I am absolutely just overjoyed that not only is my indoor air quality greatly improved but all my dust sucking appliances are now staying clean inside and not ruining themselves.
#Winning!

I tried the foam inserts and screens that slip under shingles. Heavy wooded lot with high pitch on most spots and low pitch over a small section.

Foam lasted 5 yrs. Photo attached. Never let the gutters clog but eventually they sagged and accumulated leaves, silt, sticks etc. And high flow shot over them or backed up under shingles.

My problem with other gutter covers and screens was the crappy gutter install on my house. Gutters were jammed right up to the shingles in the high spots and a steep pitch roof. Hell on the hands to install foam and impossible to install the leaf free type of guards that slip under shingles. These were great!! Cleaning gutters took longer than installing guards. Simply slipped them in the gutters and snapped on the front lip. On many of the sections I had to lift up the shingles to drive in the rear screw but no problem on a 12 12 roof with a sh!try gutter install. I plan to blow off with a blower attachment once a year (30 minutes start to finish on 4k sq ft house) to get the crap that sticks in any guard with holes or slots. Yep, that's all of them. Take your time and put these on and you'll be happy. Putting on my mother's house next. Love her but tired of hearing about the clogged gutters! These should outlast us all.

top_five_star_obscene.title: Works great, looks great..

top_five_star_obscene.text:
The impact gun is perfect to taking off and torquing down 8-12MM bolts and nuts. I would not recommend using the impact on anything higher than 12mm as the bigger hardware on cars are torqued higher than this gun can take off.

I've been mainly using the drill tool with a fine wire wheel brush to clean most contact surfaces on the engine or inside the rim's bead wall before put on new tires. It works flawlessly in removing all the crap that's stuck onto the walls.

The battery lasts quite awhile. I can use one tool almost past an entire day at work before having to switch to the secondary battery.

*****Update 04/13/2015*****
Two years and counting. These tools still perform like a champ, too bad the exterior looks like hell after all the crap I put these little things through. The batteries are still working great too.

*****Update 01/30/2017*****
Welp, the batteries that came with the kit are starting to wear out and after a full charge they don't last more than 2 days before need to recharge again. Overall, after around 4 years of usage the driver and drill are still working, but now I need to replace the batteries.

FYI, if you need new batteries. Get the model 415 version as they are the updated versions of the 413-414 batteries. The newer 415 batteries not have an increased amp rating.
top_five_star_obscene.title: Two perfect tools for a auto tech.

top_five_star_obscene.text:
Or have the thought police outlawed the word dikes too? lol. Look, I’m not an electrician. Just a regular old homeowner that likes to do as much of my own work as possible. And I’m also a guy who likes good tools. I was a machinist for many years before I retired, so I like to think that I know a little bit about good tools. Knipex is the good stuff. When it comes to any type of pliers, they are quite simply the best. Hands down.
These are some damn nice dikes. Excellent quality hardened steel. Nice ergonomic handles. Edges are extremely sharp. They easily cut right through wire, small bolts, brads, staples just about anything that you would ever reasonably expect them to. I have no idea how long the edges will maintain their razor sharpness-I probably won’t live long enough to find out. But I expect it will be a pretty long time. Look, I will confess to being a Knipex fanboy. Because they are the best. And I’m at a point in life where I don’t buy cheap crap from my enemies anymore. At least not where tools are concerned.
Are they a little bit expensive? Sure they are. But quality is always more expensive up front. Crap just keeps you paying over and over. Buy the good stuff once. You’ll never regret it.
Made in Germany 🇩🇪
Very highly recommend
Thanks for reading
